replies.title: (no subject)

The grips and other somatic triggers in lodge work are useful because you've got the impact of a lodge initiation to tap into. replies.title: Cooking chime-in
replies.content:
The farmers market in Naperville (super-fancy elite suburb) is actually pretty affordable and the vendors are from more rural parts of Illinois, Michigan, and to a lesser extent, Indiana. They aren't price gouging and their vegetables run the gamut of what grows around here, which is bountiful and beautiful but not at all exotic. What I find annoying is when farmers markets are full of people selling jewelry and non-food crafts and low on actual vegetables -- it begs the question "why do you think I'm here at the farmers market, because I don't have enough costume jewelry?"

There are two household appliances that have made my life a great deal easier as someone who cooks mostly from scratch: a pressure cooker and an air-fryer. The pressure cooker I have is a modern one, the Power Pressure Cooker XL. Modern pressure cookers like the one I have and the Instant Pot are far more foolproof and less dangerous than pressure cookers of old. You can cook beans from dry to fully cooked in an hour and a half -- it's amazing. I make soup or stew in the pressure cooker all the time. You can also cook rice in it and it takes less time than the rice cooker.

The air fryer: first, it's not frying anything. The air fryer is the grown up version of the Easy Bake Oven. It's a small convection oven. My favorite thing is to take some potatoes, wash them, cut them into wedges, spritz them with olive oil, sprinkle them with salt and pepper, and then air-fry (excuse me, BAKE) them in the air-fryer for 20 minutes. I also bake biscuits in it.

Hope this helps!

comments.title: proto-revival?
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

Last week I'd brought up Jung mentioning that Paracelsus was accused of being a druid, and asked whether there might have been some kind proto-revival taking place. You asked me for a cite, so here it is!

"And yet we know from the testimony of Conrad Gessner, of Zurich, that the very doctors whom Paracelsus attacked shunned magic on religious grounds and accused him and his pupils of sorcery. Writing to Crato von Crafftheim about Paracelsus' pupil Adam von Bodenstein, Gessner says: 'I know that most people of this kind are Arians and deny the divinity of Christ...Oporin in Basel, once a pupil of Theophrastus and his private assistant [familiaris], reported strange tales concerning the latter's intercourse with demons. They are given to senseless astrology, geomancy, necromancy, and other forbidden arts. I myself suspect that they are the last of the Druids, those of the ancient Celts who were instructed for several years in underground places by demons. It is also certain that such things are done to this very day at Salamanca in Spain. From this school also arose the wandering scholars, as they are commonly called. The most famous of these was Faust, who died not so long ago.'" (Jung 119)

Jung, CG (1942). Paracelsus as a Spiritual Phenomenon. In "Volume 13 of the Collected Works of C.G. Jung: Alchemical Studies" (pp. 109-32). New York, NY: Bollingen.

So what do you think? Is it all just late medieval Christian mudslinging (not only is he hanging out with demons, he's an Arian AND a Druid?!?!?!) ? Or is it possible some kind of proto-revival was actually happening in Salamanca?

Thanks as always for hosting this! Hope you had a blessed Alban Arthan!

Ryan M.

replies.title: Re: proto-revival?
replies.content:
Many thanks for this! Salamanca was the most important university town in Spain and a major focus of Arabic studies in the Middle Ages, and that usually included astrology and geomancy. It's quite possible that there was a thriving subculture of occultists there in the 16th century, when Gessner was writing. As far as I know nobody was reviving Druidry there, but magic -- well, that's another matter.

Though it's fair to say that the idea of Druids as practitioners of magic was common in 16th century Europe. The anonymous Arbatel of Magic has this to say about one category of magic:

"The fifth is Romane or Sibylline Magick, which acteth and operates with Tutelar Spirits and Lords, to whom the whole Orb of the earth is distributed. This is valde insignis Magia ("very remarkable magic"). To this also is the doctrine of the Druids referred."

replies.title: Re: proto-revival?
replies.content: That’s fascinating! So perhaps as texts are arriving in Salamanca from the Middle East that had been forgotten in the West, many of them are Hermetic or otherwise magical in nature, and as scholars are reading them and trying some of the practices described therein, perhaps Gessner is just looking up from his copy of de bello Gallico and saying “wait a second, I know what this is about... you people are druids!”

replies.title: Re: proto-revival?
replies.content: Very likely yes!

comments.title: Science
comments.content:
JMG,

1. I dont have any academic training, and I get the feeling when older occult texts use the term "science" or especially "sciences" that they mean something different than the current usage. Is this the case?

2. Also when the term "reason" is used in Levi's Dogma and Ritual I'm not sure what he is talking about concretely. Can you help here? Does he mean the capacity to "use reason" as a thinking tool?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Science
replies.content:
1) That's quite correct. The word "science" only took on its modern meaning late in the 19th century. Before then it meant any body of systematic knowledge. Theology, for example, was considered to be a science.

2) Here again, this word had meanings that have trickled away over the years. Reason -- ratio in Latin -- is the ability to think in a logical, directed fashion. Back in the day the idea was that everyone had mens, mind -- that's the capacity to experience thinking. You had to learn ratio, reason -- that's the ability to think in a logical and deliberate manner. If you combined this with spiritual exercises you might be granted, as a gift of divine grace, intellectus, intelligence, which is what we'd now call intuition -- the ability to perceive truths in a sudden leap of knowledge. Of course all three words get used very vaguely these days.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You've already taken the most important step by realizing that what she did was a bad idea. At intervals in this life you'll be given the opportunity to make similar mistakes, and every time you refuse to do so, you release part of that karma.

Basically there are three ways to deal with karma. You can clear it away through suffering, through willed effort, and through spiritual awakening. If you ignore it or try to run from it, you get to do it the first way, which hurts. If you face it squarely and try to choose wisely instead, you get to do it the second way, which is laborious but less painful. The third way happens when it happens -- you don't choose when or how to become enlightened. So reflect on her mistakes, choose otherwise in this life, and you'll deal with it constructively.

replies.title: Re: Protestant invocation
replies.content:
Sure. Every day, read a chapter of the Bible and pray.

My teacher in these things recommended starting with the Gospels, not with Genesis, and said that you should read the New Testament and the Psalms through three or four times before you try the rest of the Old Testament -- and even so, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy are really hard slogging. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Idries Shah liked to claim that everything and its mother-in-law was influenced by Sufism. If the legend of the Fama Fraternitatis, the original Rosicrucian manifesto, is correct, the founder of the Rosicrucians traveled to Dhamar in Yemen (misspelled as "Damcar" in some sources) and may have studied with Sufis or proto-Sufis there. Other than that, I don't know of any evidence supporting Shah's claim.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That sounds like Shah!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There is a tiny grain of truth in it, but not a direct influence on Rosicrucians. During the Medieval Era, Sufi orders (like Ismaili Order) kept some of the surviving Neoplatonic texts, which were transmitted to Knights Templar in the Crusades. Also, Sabbatean cult of Crypto-Jewish mystics in Ottoman Empire were connected to both some Sufi orders (like Mevlevis, Bektashis and Melamis) and Asiatic Brethren. Conversely, many of the Ottoman Sufis were initiated into Memphis-Misraim Rite of Freemasonry in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Hi JMG,

I think people like Idries Shah and Gurdjieff are byproducts of the negative side effects of the Theosophical Society on Western esotericism. On the one hand, Theosophical Society accomplished remarkably good deeds like popularising the occult philosophy in Western countries; but on the other hand, they created an exaggerated image of "Oriental Wisdom" which caused a fallacious belief that "Eastern countries are the true and only sources of spirituality, and everything that comes from the East is full of wisdom". Of course, we can learn a lot from Eastern traditions, but our Western tradition is rich enough, despite all the censorship and oppression that it received in the Medieval Age. It is sad to see some Western people who turn their back to this rich tradition, and blindly accepting all sorts of dogmas and rituals that come from the East without even doubting whether these are suitable for their bodies and lifestyles.
Also, I wonder how many of the Western occultists question why Eastern occult traditions were not persecuted by the ruling classes of the Medieval empires in the East or whether those ruling classes had vested interests in protecting them.

comments.title: Visualisation and Discursive Meditation
comments.content:
Dear JMG

Several questions since this will be the last MM for a few weeks!

1. This is a data point - I’ve only been doing discursive meditation for a few weeks (and not every day), but it’s interesting how that particular seated posture has become comfortable now when it was so uncomfortable and awkward at the beginning. Now if I am slumping or sitting in a seat where it is difficult to sit like that, I actually find it difficult to meditate.

2. I have an intensive aikido martial arts practice. I’m a relative newbie but I train a lot. One of the tools I’m using is daily visualization of the movements and techniques. I literally close my eyes and imagine myself doing each technique in real time and I try to make it as realistic as possible and add visual and auditory details and if possible smells/sounds. I don’t speed it up in my mind - I try to go in real time (so it’s quite time consuming - typically 20-25 min but I could easily do an hour or more - it’s pretty tiring mentally). Most importantly, I try to remember the visualization tips from the noted martial artist Peter Ralston that I should actually imagine *doing* the techniques from the first person perspective - NOT of *watching myself do it* as a third person perspective - he says it’s much more beneficial and “real” to do it from first person perspective.

My question is: do you have any more tips for visualization? Or anywhere you’ve written about visualization in more detail I can look at? I know there have been questions before where you’ve written brief answers about how to visualize better in rituals?

3. Related to 1 and 2 above: can I do my aikido visualizations *as* my discursive meditation and get the same benefits as “normal” discursive meditation? It would save me a lot of time to do one thing instead of two! I’ve been experimenting with this for a few days and I get some of the same benefits of being “immersed” and also find it easier to concentrate and focus without distraction on the visualizations (not because my visualizations are that great but because I know the techniques and I’m very interested in the subject matter).

(I am the same person who asked for advice about discursive meditation a few months ago and asked if I could do the meditation about mundane subjects that I wanted to focus on - for example whether I could meditate on how to resolve a particularly tricky situation at work etc. You told me that I could do that, but meditating on magical or symbolic subjects had the additional benefit of teaching me how to think symbolically and trained the mind to think “magically” so to speak).

How do martial arts visualizations compare?

4. I have only tried it once or twice and that briefly, but when I’ve tried to meditate on “magical” subjects, I literally have no idea what I’m doing or how to do it. To the point where I don’t know how to progress at all. For example, the first couple of lines of the Bible - I just read it and think..”Okay? So what do I think about next?” I just don’t know how to do it right?

replies.title: Re: Visualisation and Discursive Meditation
replies.content:
1) That's very common.

2) That actually covers all the points I would make.

3) The answer's the same as for your earlier question. It's going to give you half the benefits of discursive meditation on symbols. If you're okay with that, by all means.

4) If you've only tried it once or twice briefly, you haven't given it a real try. (Imagine someone who'd tried shiho-nage "once or twice and that briefly"!) Again, the way you do it is hold the symbol or passage of text in your mind for a while, not thinking about it, but just regarding it. Then see what questions come up. Let's say you're doing discursive meditation with the first verse of the Gospel of John: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." What beginning? What is the Word? How can it be both with God, and God? Why does John begin a bio of Jesus this way? How does this relate to other things you know about God? And so on. Choose one of those questions -- just one per session -- and think about it, probing it in different directions and from different points of view, until you either reach some conclusion or find yourself stymied. Don't give up easily.

The first few dozen times you do this will be like the first few dozen times you did shiho-nage: it will feel clumsy, forced, awkward. Then you'll start getting into the flow of it, and the secrets of the technique will start revealing themselves to you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When I see some occultists who complain about science, it startles me. Most of the pioneers of the Scientific Revolution (like Galileo, Copernicus, Newton, Bacon) were involved in occultism, and at their time they saw it as a way to understand the "mind of the God", as shown in the book "The Forbidden Universe" by Lynn Picknett & Clive Prince. Why do some of the modern occultists despise science? Isn't there any way to bridge the gap between science and occultism?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The difficulty is that so many scientists despise occultism, and spread the most absurd misconceptions about it. Have you ever read the notorious book Objections to Astrology edited by Paul Kurtz, the late founder of CSICOP? There are schools of astrology that make it required reading for students, because there's no better proof that scientists who condemn astrology literally don't know the first thing about it, and aren't willing to learn. The same thing is just as common when alternative health care (a common interest of occultists) comes up; a very large number of scientists insist at the top of their lungs that any healing modality that doesn't boost the profits of the pharmaceutical industry is sheer quackery, and will deploy the most embarrassing logical fallacies to shout down alternative health care.

Now of course none of that justifies the common occultist rejection of science in any logical sense, but occultists are after all human beings, and are no more likely than anyone else to turn a sympathetic ear to the scientists when so many scientists are yelling insults at them. If more scientists were willing to get down off their collective high horse and be a little more openminded about the possibility that maybe, just maybe, the five thousand year history of Western occultism has turned up a few things they don't know about yet, I suspect they'd find that occultists were ready to meet them much more than halfway.

From an occult perspective, after all, science deals solely with the material plane, the densest of the planes of being. As a set of tools for figuring out how the material plane works, science is very impressive, and it's accomplished a lot -- but those tools don't work anything like as well when applied to the other six planes of being. (This is why the replicability crisis is so insoluble in fields such as psychology, which have to deal with the inner planes at secondhand; using the scientific method on the mind is like trying to use a hacksaw to pound nails.) Occultism properly speaking deals with the next three planes -- the etheric, astral, and mental planes -- and religion deals with the three innermost planes -- the spiritual, causal, and divine planes. If more people were clear on the difference, there wouldn't be anything like the current hostility among these three branches of human knowledge!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What are the causal and divine planes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The planes (metaphorically) above the spiritual plane, of course.

Different occult philosophies subdivide the planes in different ways. Dion Fortune used a system that had the physical/etheric, lower astral, upper astral, lower mental, upper mental, lower spiritual, and upper spiritual planes. In Heindel and other Rosicrucian writers, it's the physical plane, the desire plane, the mental plane, three spiritual planes, and then the plane of God. In the system that I use, the spiritual plane is the plane of consciousness; the causal plane is the plane of causes -- the fundamental patterns of causation that cascade down the planes from there; and the divine plane is, well, the plane where you find gods and goddesses.

comments.title: Magical Effectiveness of Marriage Rituals
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Thank you for hosting these, I've found it worth my while every time I've dropped in and read through the discussions. Now on to my questions.

I have noticed that in certain circles there is a rejection of marriage on the grounds that it is a patriarchal construct built to bind women to men as their property. While I fully agree that there is a long history of marriage being just that, I can't help but feel that it is still valuable and it's orientation has shifted away from that idea and toward long-term commitment and partnership as equals (I am speaking particularly of marriage in the modern United States - I know very little of marriage elsewhere). I want to build a deeper understanding of the topic, and am trying to approach it from a magical perspective, which I am not terribly educated in.

With that said, I have three related questions:
1. Are there changes in the ritual of marriage that have mirrored the shift I perceive? Presumably, if the ritual hasn't changed, the effects of the change in perspective wouldn't necessarily be enough to overwhelm the power and intention of the ritual.
2. What makes a marriage ritual effective from a magical standpoint?
3. Do you have any idea what might be behind that complete rejection of marriage, instead of the rejection of an unhealthy form of it? It seems to be me it's a case of the "the opposite of one bad idea is another bad idea", but it can be hard for me to point this out or explain it to people who reject it strongly.

DutyBound

replies.title: Re: Magical Effectiveness of Marriage Rituals
replies.content:
Hi (I'm not JMG),

I'd like to point out that in traditional societies it was also in the best interest of women to get married in order to be protected and provided for. If you check out history (or some of our contemporaries in other parts of the world) concerning the status of unmarried women, you'll see that they end up quite reliably at the rock bottom of society.

Concerning the idea of partnership I'll leave some musings from the Middle East c. 400 BC:

Ecclesiastes 4:9-12 New International Version (NIV)

Two are better than one, because they have a good return for their labor:

If either of them falls down, one can help the other up. But pity anyone who falls and has no one to help them up.

Also, if two lie down together, they will keep warm. But how can one keep warm alone?

Though one may be overpowered, two can defend themselves. A cord of three strands is not quickly broken.

replies.title: Re: Magical Effectiveness of Marriage Rituals
replies.content:
I am suddenly reminded of the Klingon wedding ritual invented for star trek, in which they re-enact their creation myth. The first two klingon hearts created by the gods are jealous of each other's powers and battle each other, but soon realize that "if we join together, no force can stop us."

"And when the two hearts began to beat together, they filled the heavens with a terrible sound. For the first time, the gods knew fear."

The same thing but with a decidedly klingon flavor.

replies.title: Re: Magical Effectiveness of Marriage Rituals
replies.content:
1) Sure. The wife used to promise to obey the husband, and not vice versa. That's been dropped, and at this point the formal structure of marriage is a union of equals.

2) The intention of the people making the commitment, and the use of a traditional form.

3) A pervasive ideology of individual entitlement that insists that any limitation on the deracinated ego is bad. Yes, it's basically an example of the opposite of one bad idea being another bad idea. As for trying to point this out to people, don't waste your breath; people will believe what they want to believe.

replies.content:
From a purely theoretical perspective, I think there are two possible ways to determine lower planes: the first is to make contact with entities which extend further down, much as the Fay stop at the etheric plane and can contact us. This has the drawback of requiring us to make contact with beings in such a plane, which requires we recognize them, and additionally that we figure out how to communicate with them. However, if we can pull this off, we would, in theory, be able to get information about how the lower planes act; as well as information about what extends downwards and what ends at the physical plane.

The other possibility is to look for things which are more fixed: it seems plausible to me that much as our etheric bodies can cross running water, while etheric entities can't, there may be similar processes at work where things extend below the physical plane. How this would manifest is a very open ended question, and quite a bit of work and effort would be needed to even begin the process, but it seems like it could be possible.

If either is possible, I'd expect it to take centuries to be able to decipher what it all means, but in theory, if there are lower planes I think a systematic search could reveal them. Given that it seems more likely they exist than not on first principles (I doubt human beings are special, and it would astonish me if this is the lowest plane), I may have a new project to work on!

comments.title: Some questions
comments.content:
I) What do you think of René Guénon? I was reading about synarchism from an earlier comment (not my cup of tea, but very interesting), and a little further down the internet rabbit hole I stumbled on mention of him. He seems like an interesting thinker.

III a) Beginning in my middle/high school days of playing violin and fiddle, and recently rekindled by your mention here and there about mountain dulcimers, I have a big interest in stringed instruments, expecially the odd, old, and foreign. Was just recently given a mountain dulcimer in fact, and I'm working on learning it! Now I also want a hurdy-gurdy and a Hungarian zither...

Anyways, you've also mentioned that the main performance art in dark age societies is epic poetry musically recited with the accompaniment of a simple stringed instrument. Do you have any sources that describe this in more detail, or even how this is/was done? I've only been able to find a few scholarly scraps, such as this and this (do ignore the video, this is just for the audio, of Beowulf being performed in Old English, accompanied to a stringed instrument). It's a bit odd, but I have the creative urge to eventually write an epic poem, with mountain dulcimer accompaniment... An eldritch epic poem, of course! ;-)

replies.title: Re: Some questions
replies.content:
Epic poetry in its oral form (that is, before being fixed in writing), seems to have been flexible in a precisely structured way, so that successive performances by the same bard could vary greatly in their length. The mechanism by which bards used to do this was discovered by scholarship only in the early 20th century, through close examination of a living bardic tradition in what was then Yugoslavia. The most accessible account of this was written by Albert Bates Lord, "The Singer of Tales" (1960). What these bard do is not any sort of improvisation, as we know it today in performing arts; rather, they apply a fairly complex set of rules dealing with meter, with theme, and with plot to generate an infinity of recognizably similar performances of essentially the same "poem,"each adapted to the needs, interests and time-constraints of the audience before which they are signing the epic on that occasion. This complex system of rules is learned by doing only; it is nowhere codified in verbal or symbolic form.

You can see traces of this in the Old Norse poetic Edda, in those very few cases where the "same" poem has been preserved in two or more independent manuscripts (that is, in manuscripts not copied from one another or from some lost written ancestral manuscript). The independent texts of such an Eddic poem differ from one another in just this sort of way, suggesting that Eddic poetry was performed with much the same sort of structured flexibility. (Scholarly editions of the Edda level these differences out in their quest for a non-existent "true original text" of, say, the "Voluspa" or the "Havamal"--as if such a thing had ever existed in old Scandinavia.)

I am no singer or musician, but I used to do oral tale-telling, and I soon found out that somewhat the same sort of precisely structured flexibility can be achieved in that art as well.

comments.title: Sacred Geometry - follow on
comments.content:
Last Monday, I referred to "By Hand & Eye" and this week, I note James Kunstler is again talking about bad architecture.
The book explains how virtually everything pre-industrialism has a rhythm based on whole-number divisions, for example, that most larger buildings up until the 1940s are 5 or 6 storeys and have set height for the upper floors and a 1.5 a ground floor scaled to 1.5 times the height of the upper floors. The height of the upper floors is also relative to the width and depth of the building (to a point). This gives a balanced feel to the buildings.
We have a constructed landscape of buildings with unfamiliar and usually confusing, though sometimes elegantly beautiful, forms, lack of beauty or decoration, with no relation to human scale. There is no rhythm in these buildings, i.e. the canon of architecture that made the bottom floor 1.5 times the height of the upper floors. There is no whole-number relationship between floor height, width, building height, and depth, which was the case for centuries. We basically abandoned and forgot the sacred geometry of ratios in our built-up world.
Another realization -- from your Ecosophia post about the decline of Neopaganism and concomitant disintegration of Social Justice Activism -- was that the zeitgeist seems to be one of a society under enormous psychic stress.
I was wondering if this psychic stress might not be partly due to, or at least exacerbated by, the fact that since the 1930s, we stopped using principles of sacred geometry in our built structures?

Bruce
Renaissance Man.

Whole number ratios and meaningful geometries are among the things that made older architecture beautiful and pleasant to live in, and their absence is among the things that make modern architecture ugly and unpleasant to live in.

replies.title: Re: Exorcism

That's difficult and challenging work, and it requires a thorough knowledge of the branch of spiritual work we occultists call "magic." The Malleus Maleficarum won't give you that -- it's mostly a manual on how to manufacture panic so you can kill people with impunity.

If you're a Christian, I recommend starting out with Gareth Knight's book Experience of the Inner Worlds -- Knight's a practicing Anglican Christian and his book is entirely oriented to people who share his Christian faith. There are practices, which you'll need to take up and do daily. Another valuable work of Christian magic is Peter Roche de Coppens' The Nature and Use of Ritual, which discusses the practical use of the Lord's Prayer, the recitation of the Apostle's Creed, and various other central Christian acts of faith. That should get you started.

comments.title: Saving a failing Oak tree from last week's Magic Monday
comments.content:
This isn’t a question, but a response to the guy last week who was mourning having to cut down a large Oak tree.  I have no other way of getting hold of him. 

Another possibility, if the old Oak’s location on the property will allow it, is to have it cut off as close to the ground as the chainsaw will allow and DO NOT allow the arborist to put herbicide or anything on the stump, and DO NOT grind the stump.  A professional tree service will probably not like this idea -  Ignore them.  Keep leaf litter and soil away from the short stump and leave it alone.  Often times, Oaks can grow back from a cut stump. Keep it watered if it is dry.  It will send up several suckers in the spring to try to feed the massive root system, let all of them grow for a few years to feed the roots and then pick  the straightest one and prune all of the others.  Keep a fence around the stump to protect from deer and rabbits for the first few years. After 10-15 years, a presentable little Oak tree will be there, which is in fact your old tree, with a new body.   This may not work, but it might, you could ask the tree what it thinks.  I used to own land in northern Missouri and there were numerous young Oaks of several species that had grown off of old stumps.  They sprout from the outer edge near the cambium/bark area not the center of the old trunk.   Your Pin Oak is in the Red Oak family. 

There are several websites that address this, and it may even start becoming a thing in forest regeneration in the Ozarks of Missouri.  I am a literal “tree hugger,” with a particular fondness for Oaks and love the ideas of saving and keeping the old root system going if at all possible.  Much of that Oak’s wisdom comes from its root system and the mycorrhizae(that is a whole other fascinating realm) .  It was my interaction with the Oaks on my land that led me to Google druidry, I found JMG and have been reading his work since 2009.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
To one and all, happy Solstice, Christmas, Hanukkah or any other holiday you may be celebrating.

I made a lengthy post back in March discussing my experience of an, um, shall we say, incommodious spirit. At that time I was in the very early stages of beginning to process a severe physical and emotional trauma, namely being chemically castrated by a prescription medication given to me, purportedly for acne, at the age of 14. Yes, I know that sounds incredible, but it happened. Google search for Isotretinoin if you want to learn more about the delightful substance in question, still prescribed without restriction to kids to this day.

Having previously begun a magical practice back when I was still in the thicket of confusion and denial about the abovementioned trauma but then shelved it after becoming concerned it was contributing to me feeling dissociated, I decided at this time to resume the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram in the hope it would help cleanse me of some of the mental crud accumulated over the previous 12 years. Instead, within a few days I found myself encountering a force or presence that felt to me like the spirit of the acne drug. It had a sort of sickly, grubby energy and seemed to dwell inside me and yet also be somewhat separate, with its own agency.

Having never experienced anything like this before, I was very frightened, fearing I'd attracted a demon. I posted on here asking for help and your advice, Mr. Greer, was that, while you didn't think it was a demon - speculating that it might be the egregor of the drug - I should drop magic immediately and continue to leave it well alone for the rest of my life.

So I did that - well, I dropped it, at least. The question of my commitment to lifelong apostasy, I'm coming to.

Fast-forward to now. The past 9 months have brought much healing. I've finally come to some level of acceptance about what happened to me as a young teenager, and what it's left me with. The intense feelings of anger, pain and loss that flared up as I began confronting the trauma have mostly subsided into the background and after a 12-yearlong nightmare I feel able, cautiously, to begin to live a life again, to try to make the most of what I am.

The spirit hasn't reared its head much since that initial shocking encounter and I wonder if the greater peace I now feel with my 'sickness', my growing acceptance of it as part of me, has lessened the power the spirit might have had over me? Nevertheless, yesterday I was out for a walk out in the neighbourhood and I ran into what seemed to me a quite unpleasant presence. It seemed to sort of stick its tongue out at me - not, it occurs to me, all that dissimilar to your common or garden non-disembodied delinquent. Reflecting on this I begin to feel like I'm in a bit of a sticky situation. My eyes, it seems, have been permanently opened to the spirit world. It feels unwise to simply stand exposed and not avail myself of some sort of prophylactic against spirits that might wish to make mischief with me. Yet, Mr. Greer, your previous advice was unequivocal and I feel it would be equally unwise of me to ignore it.

In the wake of the encounter with that initial spirit, I seriously considered turning to Christianity as a non-magical refuge, but ultimately couldn't overcome my intellectual and spiritual aversion to its (as it seems to me) oversimplified dualism. I find similar problems with the other exoteric religions and however it might serve me pragmatically, I couldn't embrace any of them in good faith.

My two-part question, then, is this: one, do you think I might safely and effectively use natural magic to ward off astral ne'er-do-wells, and if so what specifically? Two, based on your evaluation of me, does your opinion remain that I definitely definitely shouldn't ever touch ritual magic again?

Thanks for reading.

Al

I'm going to encourage you to experiment with a different ritual -- the Sphere of Protection -- which you can find here (scroll down to the bottom to start). That should give you ample protection. I'm glad you're doing better.

replies.title: meditation on the Bible
replies.content:
Some of the books in the Jewish Bible (what Protestants call the Old Testament) are light on theology; a couple of them don't even mention God. If I understand the purpose of discursive meditiation, you aren't obligated to meditate on every single verse or chapter in a book.

Here is a list of the books I think might work the best for you. They aren't long books; you can read through any one of them, decide if you want to make use of it for meditation, and if it doesn't appeal, read another.

Proverbs (Wisdom literature)

Song of Songs (in some Christian Bibles, this is called the Song of Solomon). Erotic poetry with themes from nature, perhaps composed for singing at weddings.

Jonah (read this story as you would a myth from any culture)

Ecclesiastes (more wisdom literature; a series of meditations on fortune, merit, and death)
